flashcards.a: Availability of sufficient food and people's economic access to purchase it. Requires both food production and purchasing power.

flashcards.q: Why improve crop and livestock production?
flashcards.a: Growing population needs more food, but cultivable land is limited. Must increase production efficiency per unit area rather than expanding land.
Carbohydrates for energy. Examples: wheat, rice, maize, millets, sorghum.
Proteins for body building and repair. Examples: gram, pea, lentil, pigeon pea, green gram, black gram.
Fats and oils for energy and cellular functions. Examples: soyabean, groundnut, sesame, mustard, linseed, sunflower.
Crops grown in monsoon/rainy season (June-October). Examples: paddy, soyabean, pigeon pea, maize, cotton, green gram, black gram.
Crops grown in winter season (November-April). Examples: wheat, gram, peas, mustard, linseed.
Duration of sunlight exposure. Critical for plant growth and flowering through photosynthesis. Different crops require different photoperiods.

flashcards.q: Name the three stages of farming for crop improvement.
flashcards.a: Stage 1: Choice of seeds for planting. Stage 2: Nurturing of crop plants. Stage 3: Protection of growing and harvested crops.
Crossing between genetically dissimilar plants to combine desirable traits. Types: intervarietal, interspecific, intergeneric.

flashcards.q: What is genetic modification of crops?
flashcards.a: Directly introducing specific genes providing desired characteristics (herbicide resistance, pest resistance) into crops.
Nutrients required in large quantities: nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, calcium, magnesium, sulphur. Obtained from soil.
Nutrients required in small quantities: iron, manganese, boron, zinc, copper, molybdenum, chlorine. Essential for plant health.
Organic material from decomposed animal excreta and plant waste. Improves soil structure, water-holding capacity, and provides slow-release nutrients.
Commercially produced concentrated plant nutrients. Provide quick nutrient availability. Main nutrients: nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium.
Agricultural system with minimal/no chemicals. Uses organic manures, recycled waste, bio-agents, and healthy cropping systems (mixed, intercropping, rotation).
Growing two or more crops simultaneously on same land. Reduces failure risk through crop diversification. Example: wheat + gram.
Growing two or more crops in definite row patterns with different nutrient requirements. Maximizes resource use and prevents pest spread.
Growing different crops in pre-planned succession on same land. Breaks pest cycles and maintains soil fertility.
Unwanted plants in cultivated fields competing with crops for food, water, space, light. Examples: Xanthium, Parthenium, Cyperus rotundus.
Combined approach using resistant varieties, preventive practices (summer ploughing, intercropping, rotation), mechanical control, and selective chemicals.

flashcards.q: What are biotic stresses in crops?
flashcards.a: Caused by living organisms: diseases (bacteria, fungi, viruses), insects, nematodes. Reduce crop health and yield.

flashcards.q: What are abiotic stresses in crops?
flashcards.a: Environmental factors: drought, high soil salinity, waterlogging, extreme heat, cold, frost. Reduce productivity under stress conditions.

flashcards.q: Why is shorter maturity duration desirable?
flashcards.a: Enables multiple crops per year, reduces production costs, uniform maturity simplifies harvesting and reduces losses.
High-quality seeds ensure uniform germination and crop growth. Good quality requires genetic purity, uniform germination, disease-free seeds, high viability.
Biotic factors: insects, rodents, fungi, mites, bacteria. Abiotic factors: inappropriate moisture and temperature.
Strict cleaning, proper drying (sun then shade), fumigation, systematic warehouse management controlling temperature and humidity.
Supplying water to crops through artificial systems at critical growth stages. Important because most Indian agriculture is rain-fed.

flashcards.q: Name four irrigation systems used in India.
flashcards.a: Wells (dug and tube), canals, river lift systems, tanks. Modern: rainwater harvesting and check-dams.
Scientific management of livestock including feeding, breeding, shelter, and disease control. Includes cattle, poultry, fish, bee-keeping.
Cattle (cows and buffaloes) bred and managed for milk production. Milk yield depends on lactation period duration.
Cattle used for farm labor: tilling, irrigation, pulling carts. Require strength and stamina.

flashcards.q: Why cross-breed exotic and indigenous cattle?
flashcards.a: Combines high productivity from exotic breeds with disease resistance and adaptation to local climate from indigenous breeds.

flashcards.q: What are the two food requirements of dairy cattle?
flashcards.a: Maintenance requirement: food for basic health. Milk-producing requirement: additional food during lactation for milk synthesis.
Chickens raised for meat. Fast-growing, heavy breeds. Require protein-rich diet with high vitamins A and K.
Chickens raised for egg production. Lighter breeds. Require balanced diet for sustained egg production.
Obtaining fish from natural waters using fishing nets and traditional methods. Limited by natural fish stocks.
Fish farming in controlled environments (ponds, tanks). Sustainable, enables high-yield production, doesn't deplete wild populations.
Farming fish and shellfish in seawater. Includes finned fish, shellfish (prawns, mussels, oysters), seaweed.
Single pond contains 5-6 fish species with different feeding habits. Catlas (surface), Rohu (middle), Mrigals/Carps (bottom), Grass Carp (weeds). No competition, maximum yield.

flashcards.q: Why is hormonal stimulation breeding important for fish?
flashcards.a: Enables artificial breeding year-round, producing pure fish seed in desired quantities. Overcomes natural monsoon-only breeding limitation.
Farming bees for honey and wax production. Requires low investment, serves as additional farm income.
Flowering plants available to bees for nectar and pollen. Quality and quantity determine honey value, taste, and production.
High honey collection capacity, reduced stinging, long hive habitation, excellent breeding. Italian bee (A. mellifera) possesses these traits.

flashcards.q: True or False: Hybridisation uses molecular techniques to insert genes.
flashcards.a: False. Hybridisation crosses whole plants. Genetic modification uses molecular techniques to insert specific genes.

flashcards.q: True or False: Manure provides long-term soil benefits than fertilizers.
flashcards.a: True. Manure improves soil structure and microbial activity long-term; fertilizers provide short-term nutrients.

flashcards.q: True or False: Monoculture is more resistant to pests than diverse cropping.
flashcards.a: False. Monoculture is highly vulnerable—pests specialized for one crop spread easily. Diversity increases resistance.

flashcards.q: True or False: Rain-fed agriculture requires no irrigation.
flashcards.a: True. Rain-fed agriculture depends entirely on rainfall. Irrigation supplements water-scarce regions.

flashcards.q: True or False: Indigenous cattle breeds are more productive than exotic breeds.
flashcards.a: False. Exotic breeds are more productive but less disease-resistant. Indigenous breeds are hardy but less productive. Cross-breeding is optimal.

flashcards.q: True or False: Capture fishing can meet global fish demand indefinitely.
flashcards.a: False. Wild fish stocks decline due to overfishing. Aquaculture is essential for sustainable fish supply.

flashcards.q: What are the desirable factors for crop variety improvement?
flashcards.a: Higher yield, improved quality, biotic and abiotic resistance, shorter maturity duration, wider adaptability, desirable agronomic characteristics.

flashcards.q: Why is dwarfness desirable in cereals?
flashcards.a: Shorter plants consume fewer nutrients, easier harvesting, less lodging in wind/rain. Increases productivity per plant.

flashcards.q: Why is tallness desirable in fodder crops?
flashcards.a: Greater height increases biomass and forage available for livestock feed per plant.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between food availability and food security?
flashcards.a: Food security requires BOTH availability (sufficient production) AND access (people's purchasing power). Either alone is insufficient.

flashcards.q: How do preventive pest control methods reduce chemical dependency?
flashcards.a: Using resistant varieties, summer ploughing, intercropping, crop rotation, and mechanical removal controls pests without/with minimal pesticides.

flashcards.q: True or False: All nutrients come from soil.
flashcards.a: False. Air provides carbon and oxygen; water provides hydrogen and oxygen; soil provides 13 additional nutrients.

flashcards.q: What is the principle underlying composite fish culture?
flashcards.a: Ecological stratification—different species utilize different ecological niches without competing, maximizing total resource use.

flashcards.q: How does crop rotation maintain soil fertility?
flashcards.a: Legume crops fix nitrogen in soil, replenishing it. Different crops utilize different soil nutrients, preventing depletion.

flashcards.q: True or False: Shorter crop cycle always means higher annual productivity.
flashcards.a: True for land utilization. Shorter cycle enables multiple crops annually from same land.

flashcards.q: What factors determine appropriate variety selection for a farmer?
flashcards.a: Local climate, water availability, soil conditions, pest/disease prevalence, market demand, farmer resources. Variety should match specific conditions.
